title: Window frame photo mounting
subtitle: Cutting and mounting a paper window to present a photograph in a scrapbook page
excerpt: A window mount is a frame cut from card or heavy paper with an opening slightly smaller than the photograph, which is then positioned behind the window so the image shows through. In scrapbooking, a window mount lifts the photograph off the page, creates a visual border, and allows the photograph to be removed and replaced without re-gluing.
sourceNotes: Window mount cutting technique synthesised from photography presentation and scrapbooking practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A board or paper frame with a rectangular opening cut into it, through which a photograph or artwork is viewed. The opening is slightly smaller than the image so the mount covers the image edges. Standard in gallery framing and scrapbooking.

A cut made at an angle (typically 45 degrees) through the mount, so the edge of the window opening is angled rather than square. In hand cutting, a straight knife cut produces a square edge; a bevel requires a special angle jig or a very steady hand.

content.text: at home uses cartridge paper or thin card rather than museum board, but the construction principle is the same as professional framing: an opening that reveals the image while the mount covers the margins. The photograph sits behind the mount, held in place with photo corners.

The window opening should be 3 to 5 mm smaller than the photograph on all four sides. This overlap hides the photograph edges (which are sometimes uneven) and ensures the photo cannot slip through the opening. Mark the opening lightly in pencil on the back of the mount. The mount border should be equal on three sides and slightly deeper at the bottom, the same optical principle as classical page margins.
Cut along the four lines of the opening with the craft knife against the metal ruler. This produces a square-edged window. A

at 45 degrees gives a more refined edge but requires a special jig. For the corners: cut each line through the corner point so the four cuts overlap slightly. The waste piece lifts out cleanly. A corner that is slightly undercut is easier to fix than one that has been overcut.

Slide photo corners onto the four corners of the photograph. Position the photograph behind the window opening so the image is centred, then press the photo-corner adhesive tabs onto the back of the mount. The photograph can now be removed and replaced by sliding it out of the corners.

Glue the mount to the scrapbook page with PVA on the back of the mount, or attach with photo corners for a fully removable assembly. A narrow border of washi tape around the outer edge of the mount ties it to the page layout without full adhesion. Write the caption, date, or place name below the window in the same pen used elsewhere on the spread.
